13.1.label: Question 13.1
13.1.scenario:
A country's legislation for the supervision of its short-term health insurance industry is being reviewed.

To try and avoid a situation where a short-term insurer cannot pay all valid claims the following suggestions have been made:

(a) The solvency capital requirement should be proportional to technical reserves.
(b) The solvency capital requirement should be proportional to the square root of premium income.
(c) There should be a statutory basis for the calculation of technical reserves.
(d) All technical reserves plus the solvency capital requirement should be covered by government securities.
(e) All reinsurance should be placed with a state-owned reinsurance company.

(i).synopsis: Minimum free reserves with asset regulations (permitted assets, valuation bases) and liability regulations (valuation bases, actuarial sign-off); financial restrictions (minimum premiums, outgo restrictions, statutory reinsurance levels, maximum premium income for given capital); public information (prospective business plans, retrospective detailed reports); approval required for types of business and policy conditions; regulatory advice from actuaries and regular checks; fit and proper criteria for directors; marketing and selling restrictions
(i).question: Explain briefly the main aspects of a short-term health insurance company and its business that a supervisory authority could control to ensure that the company would be able to fulfil its obligations to policyholders.

Main aspects controlled by supervisory a: Note: ensuring that the company can meet its obligations to policyholders may be very difficult (impossible?) to achieve. However, the following suggestions may help.
The company must maintain a minimum level of free reserves, including: [½]
• assets:
(a) regulations on assets that may be held (or counted) [½]
(b) set bases for valuing assets [½]
• liabilities:
(a) set bases for valuing (might be difficult, or inappropriate in many cases) [1]
(b) actuarial sign-off of reserves [½]
The supervisory authority could insist upon other financial restrictions, such as:
• minimum premiums or premiums must be approved by the regulators (ensures sufficient funds coming in, but very difficult to implement especially where individual underwriting is concerned) [½]
• restrictions on certain items of outgo (dividends, expense levels) [½]
• statutory levels of reinsurance [½]
• maximum level of premium income for a given level of capital. [½]
Companies must make information publicly available, such as:
• prospective: regular business plans for approval by authority (and company can only operate within limits agreed within the plan) [1]
• retrospective: detailed reports on the business over the last accounting period(s). [½]
Approval must be required, for:
• types of business written [½]
• policy conditions. [½]
The regulator may collect information or take advice from actuaries and other skilled persons [½]
and use this to regularly check that a company is being run properly, with due regard to the risks that it faces. [½]
Individuals in specific positions (eg directors) must be approved and satisfy 'fit and proper' criteria. [½]
The regulators may impose restrictions and requirements on marketing and selling of health insurance products. [½]
[Maximum 6]

(ii).synopsis: Reasons — (a) proportional to TR: appropriate as SCR cushions fluctuations in TR value, more suitable for stable/declining premium income. (b) proportional to sqrt(premium): consistent with credibility/statistical theory, variation in total claims grows with sqrt of premium income, gives suitable minimum for new/growing companies. (c) statutory basis for TR: prevents SCR requirements being met by weakening reserving basis, promotes consistency but hard to set appropriate bases for unusual risks. (d) TR+SCR covered by government securities: ensures assets are secure against investment failure, provides government funding though below market rates. (e) state-owned reinsurance: provides protection without reinsurer failure risk, but cost may be expensive and discourage take-up. Suitability — criteria include: weak reserving basis could meet SCR (not c); new/growing company needs strong minimum margin (b ok, a not); contracting company needs margin for outstanding claims (a ok, b not); credibility theory is a pure approach (b may not be fully appropriate); different portfolios have different ruin probabilities not incorporated in any suggestion; SCR should allow for asset valuation basis and quality (only d); actual investments should reflect liability type and term (d restrictive, results in lower investment returns and higher premiums); no suggestion enforces adequate premium rates; legislation needs tailoring to risk type
(ii).question: State reasons for each of the suggestions, and comment on their suitability.

Reasons: (a) Solvency capital requirement proportional to technical reserves

One of the purposes of solvency capital is to provide a cushion for fluctuations in the value of assets or liabilities (technical reserves). As fluctuations are generally proportional to the amounts, it might be appropriate to have the solvency capital proportional to the amount of the technical reserves. [1]
More suitable for a company with stable (or declining) premium income. [½]
(b) Solvency capital requirement proportional to square root of premium income

This is consistent with statistical theory; the variation in the total claim amount, allowing for variation in the number of claims, increases roughly in line with the square root of premium income. [½]
Also, the basis does give a suitable minimum for new and growing companies and ignores distortions caused by the strength of the valuation basis. [½]
(c) Statutory basis for calculating technical reserves

It is necessary / desirable as free reserves are assets less technical reserves. This basis prevents the possibility of the capital requirements being satisfied through weakening the reserving basis. [1]
This aims to provide consistency between companies but sometimes it can be hard to establish appropriate statutory reserving bases, especially for unusual risks. [½]
(d) Technical reserves + solvency capital invested in government securities

This should ensure that the assets are secure, so there is no danger of policyholder claims not being met through investment failure. (But if these were only fixed-interest investments, monetary security would not protect against high inflation.) [½]
Also it provides the government with funding, but the government may not pay market rates of interest. This would result in lower security for the policyholder. [½]

Reinsurance provides protection for the insurer. With a State-owned reinsurer there should be no question regarding the failure of reinsurers (which might happen from time to time if not State-owned). [1]
Also, you need to consider the cost of reinsurance. If the reinsurance rates are expensive this would discourage the insurer from taking out reinsurance. [½]
Suitability: The suitability of each suggestion can be considered against the following criteria. Note that the final outcome might be a combination of the suggestions.
Solvency capital requirements might be met by the company using a weak reserving basis; [½]
applies to all suggestions except (c), and especially for (a). [½]
Some judgement might be appropriate within the reserving basis to allow for peculiar circumstances [½]
and so (c) is not ideal. [½]
A new or fast growing company needs a strong minimum margin; [½]
(b) is OK, but (a) is not. [½]
A contracting company (or one writing no new business) needs a margin in case outstanding claims are greater than expected; [½]
(a) is OK, but (b) is not. [½]
Credibility theory is based on a pure approach, and does not make full allowance for the vagaries of the real world; [½]
(b) may not be fully appropriate. [½]
Different portfolios of business may produce different levels of ruin probability. This is not incorporated into any of the suggestions. [½]
For example, a company writing only 10 PMI policies may satisfy all of the criteria (a) to (e), but could go bust on a single claim from any of the policies. [½]
The solvency capital requirements should make allowance for the valuation basis of the assets, and the quality of the assets. [½]
Only (d) makes any attempt to do this. [½]
The actual investments should reflect the type and term of liabilities; (d) is restrictive in this respect, and probably results in lower investment returns and hence higher premiums. [1]
None of the bases enforces adequacy of premium rates [½]
and so the company may have written unprofitable business for two years before it is spotted that the company is insolvent. [½]
The legislation may need to be tailored to the particular risks that the company faces (eg the type of business written). [½]
There is no mention of this in (a) to (e). [½]
State-owned reinsurers introduce an element of 'nationalising the insurance industry' which may not be conducive to promoting an efficient market-driven insurance industry. [½]
Is reinsurance to be compulsory, and at what level? [½]
Extension of this is a State-owned insurer (ie cut out the middle man). [½]
[Maximum 13]
